title: Border Safety and Security Act of 2025
short_title: Border Safety and Security Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.
plain_summary: This bill requires the Department of Homeland Security to stop people without proper papers from entering the United States when the government cannot hold them in detention or send them back to nearby countries like Mexico or Canada. The bill also allows the Department to stop these entries anytime they think it is needed to control the border better. If the Department does not follow these rules, individual states can take them to court. The goal is to give the government more power to control who comes into the country at the borders.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Border Safety and Security Act of twenty twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Suspension of Entry of Aliens
sections.text:
The Secretary of Homeland Security has the power to stop certain people from entering the United States at land borders or sea borders. The Secretary can use this power when they decide it is needed to gain operational control over the border. Operational control means the government can prevent people from entering the country illegally and can respond to threats at the border.

The Secretary must stop certain people from entering the country during any time when the government cannot properly detain these people as required by immigration law or cannot place them in proper monitoring programs as required by immigration law.

State attorneys general can sue the Secretary of Homeland Security in federal court if they believe the Secretary is not following the requirement to stop people from entering when the government cannot properly detain or monitor them. States can do this if the violation affects their state or their residents.

In this section, the term covered alien means a person trying to enter the United States who cannot be admitted because they do not have proper documentation under immigration law. Other terms used in this section have the same meanings as given in the Immigration and Nationality Act.
